**Woonsocket** ([/wʊnˈsɒkɪt, wən-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-Woonsocket.oga "File:En-Woonsocket.oga") [*wuun-SOK-it, wən-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[6]](#cite_note-6)), is a city in [Providence County, Rhode Island](/wiki/Providence_County,_Rhode_Island "Providence County, Rhode Island"), United States. The population was 43,240 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_Census "2020 United States Census"), making it the sixth largest city in the state. Being Rhode Island's northernmost city, Woonsocket lies directly south of the [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") state line and constitutes part of both the [Providence metropolitan area](/wiki/Providence_metropolitan_area "Providence metropolitan area") and the larger [Greater Boston](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston") [Combined Statistical Area](/wiki/Combined_Statistical_Area "Combined Statistical Area").

The city is the corporate headquarters of [CVS Health](/wiki/CVS_Health "CVS Health"), a pharmacy services provider. It is home to [Landmark Medical Center](/wiki/Landmark_Medical_Center "Landmark Medical Center"), the [Museum of Work and Culture](/wiki/Museum_of_Work_and_Culture "Museum of Work and Culture"), and the [American-French Genealogical Society](/wiki/American-French_Genealogical_Society "American-French Genealogical Society").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woonsocket,_Rhode_Island&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Historic mill villages of Woonsocket](/wiki/Historic_mill_villages_of_Woonsocket "Historic mill villages of Woonsocket")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Woonsocket_District_Courthouse_2015.jpg/250px-Woonsocket_District_Courthouse_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Woonsocket_District_Courthouse_2015.jpg)

[Woonsocket District Courthouse](/wiki/Woonsocket_District_Courthouse "Woonsocket District Courthouse"), built in 1894

Before the arrival of European settlers in northern Rhode Island during the 17th century, today's Woonsocket region was inhabited by three [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribes: the [Nipmuc](/wiki/Nipmuc "Nipmuc") (Cowesett), [Wampanoag](/wiki/Wampanoag "Wampanoag"), and [Narragansett](/wiki/Narragansett_(tribe) "Narragansett (tribe)").[[8]](#cite_note-woonsocket.org-8) In 1661, the English theologian [Roger Williams](/wiki/Roger_Williams "Roger Williams") purchased the area from the "Coweset and Nipmucks", and in a letter referred to modern day Woonsocket as *Niswosakit*.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Other possible derivations of the name include several Nipmuc geographic names from nearby [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"). These include *Woonksechocksett*, from [Worcester County](/wiki/Worcester_County,_Massachusetts "Worcester County, Massachusetts") meaning "fox country", and *Wannashowatuckqut*, also from Worcester County, meaning "at the fork of the river".[[10]](#cite_note-names-10) Another theory proposes that the city was named after [Woonsocket Hill](/wiki/Woonsocket_Hill "Woonsocket Hill") in neighboring [North Smithfield](/wiki/North_Smithfield,_Rhode_Island "North Smithfield, Rhode Island").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Woonsocket Falls Village was founded in the 1820s. Its fortunes expanded as the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") developed in nearby [Pawtucket](/wiki/Pawtucket,_Rhode_Island "Pawtucket, Rhode Island"). With the [Blackstone River](/wiki/Blackstone_River "Blackstone River") providing ample water power, the region became a prime location for [textile](/wiki/Textile "Textile") [mills](/wiki/Factory "Factory"). In 1831 [Edward Harris](/wiki/Edward_Harris_(Rhode_Island_politician) "Edward Harris (Rhode Island politician)") built his first textile mill in Woonsocket.

The town of Woonsocket was not established until 1867, when three villages in the town of [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Rhode_Island "Cumberland, Rhode Island"), namely Woonsocket Falls, Social and Jenckesville, officially became the town of Woonsocket. In 1871, [three additional industrial villages](/wiki/Historic_mill_villages_of_Woonsocket "Historic mill villages of Woonsocket")[[12]](#cite_note-MW-12) from Smithfield—Hamlet, Bernon, and Globe—were added to the town, establishing its present boundaries. Woonsocket was incorporated as city in 1888.

The growth of industries and associated jobs attracted numerous immigrants, predominantly [Québecois](/wiki/Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois_people "Québécois people") and [French-Canadians](/wiki/French-Canadians "French-Canadians") from other provinces. When the Société Saint-Jean-Baptiste d'Holyoke organized a national cultural and [benefit society](/wiki/Benefit_society "Benefit society") in 1899, the [Union Saint-Jean-Baptiste d'Amérique](/wiki/Union_Saint-Jean-Baptiste_d%E2%80%99Amerique "Union Saint-Jean-Baptiste d’Amerique"), Woonsocket, with its proximity to several industrial areas having large French-Canadian populations, was chosen for the organization's headquarters.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

By 1913, a survey by the [American Association of Foreign Language Newspapers](/wiki/American_Association_of_Foreign_Language_Newspapers "American Association of Foreign Language Newspapers") found the city had to have the sixth-largest population of French or French-Canadian foreign nationals in the country.[[14]](#cite_note-AmLeader-14) In the decades that followed this population grew, and by time the local textile industry were shuttered during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), ethnic French Canadians comprised 75 percent of the population. French-language newspapers were published; radio programs, movies, and most public conversations were conducted in French.[[15]](#cite_note-15) As recently as 1980, 70% of Woonsocket's population was of French-Canadian descent. The [New England French](/wiki/New_England_French "New England French") language their ancestors spoke gradually vanished from public discourse.[[16]](#cite_note-Anctil_p._41-16)

Throughout the 20th century the city's fortunes ebbed and flowed with national trends. During the Great Depression the textile economy of Woonsocket came to an effective standstill; however, it was revived during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The city became a major center of fabric manufacturing for the war effort, including production of military uniforms.

In the postwar years, the Woonsocket economy diversified as manufacturing declined, and other commercial sectors, such as retail, technology and financial services took hold.[[12]](#cite_note-MW-12) In the early 1980s Woonsocket was struggling with high unemployment rates.[[16]](#cite_note-Anctil_p._41-16)

Beginning in 1979, Woonsocket sponsored Autumnfest, an annual cultural festival that takes place on [Columbus Day](/wiki/Columbus_Day "Columbus Day") weekend, at World War II Veteran's Memorial State Park. It has become one of the city's most popular events, attracting thousands of attendees.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woonsocket,_Rhode_Island&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 8.0 square miles (21 km2), of which 7.7 square miles (20 km2) is land and 0.3 square miles (0.78 km2) (3.14%) is water. Woonsocket is drained by the [Blackstone River](/wiki/Blackstone_River "Blackstone River"). Adjacent communities include [Blackstone](/wiki/Blackstone,_Massachusetts "Blackstone, Massachusetts") and [Bellingham](/wiki/Bellingham,_Massachusetts "Bellingham, Massachusetts"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), along with [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Rhode_Island "Cumberland, Rhode Island") and [North Smithfield](/wiki/North_Smithfield,_Rhode_Island "North Smithfield, Rhode Island"), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woonsocket,_Rhode_Island&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

Woonsocket has a strong [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*) with four distinct seasons. Being influenced by both the sea and the interior during winter, [diurnal temperature variation](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation") is relatively high, with days most often being above freezing before severe frosts hit at night.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woonsocket,_Rhode_Island&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Woonsocket had a population of 43,240.[[21]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-22) The population density was 5,582.2 inhabitants per square mile (2,155.3/km2). There were 19,754 housing units at an average density of 2,550.2 per square mile (984.6/km2).[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) The median age was 37.2 years; 21.9% of residents were under the age of 18 and 14.5% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 95.4 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 92.7 males.[[21]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-21)

100.0% of residents lived in urban areas, while 0.0% lived in rural areas.[[24]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-24)

There were 18,287 households in Woonsocket, of which 27.8% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 30.2% were married-couple households, 24.7% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 33.9% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 34.6% of all households were made up of individuals and 11.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[21]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-21)

The average household size was 2.5 and the average family size was 3.2.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The percent of those with a bachelor’s degree or higher was estimated to be 12.4% of the population.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The 2016-2020 5-year [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimates show that the median household income was $44,310 (with a margin of error of +/- $2,816) and the median family income was $55,818 (+/- $4,785). Males had a median income of $38,889 (+/- $2,065) versus $27,544 (+/- $2,343) for females.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The median income for those above 16 years old was $33,424 (+/- $2,183).[[28]](#cite_note-28) Approximately, 15.0% of families and 21.0% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 34.8% of those under the age of 18 and 15.1% of those ages 65 or over.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woonsocket,_Rhode_Island&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

At the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"),[[5]](#cite_note-GR2-5) there were 43,224 people, 17,750 households, and 10,774 families residing in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 5,608.8 inhabitants per square mile (2,165.6/km2). There were 18,757 housing units at an average density of 2,433.9 per square mile (939.7/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 83.14% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 4.44% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.32% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 4.06% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.03% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 4.86% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 3.14% from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino of any race were 9.32% of the population.

Woonsocket is a part of the [Providence metropolitan area](/wiki/Providence_metropolitan_area "Providence metropolitan area"), which has an estimated population of 1,622,520.

There were 17,750 households, out of which 31.2% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 39.4% were married couples living together, 16.2% had a female householder with no husband present, and 39.3% were non-families. Of all households, 32.7% were made up of individuals, and 12.5% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.37 and the average family size was 3.02.

In the city, the population was spread out, with 25.8% under the age of 18, 9.2% from 18 to 24, 30.0% from 25 to 44, 19.7% from 45 to 64, and 15.2% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 35 years. For every 100 females, there were 91.2 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 86.8 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $30,819, and the median income for a family was $38,353. Males had a median income of $31,465 versus $24,638 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $16,223. About 16.7% of families and 19.4% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 31.3% of those under age 18 and 14.7% of those age 65 or over. In March 2013, *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* reported that one-third of Woonsocket's population used [food stamps](/wiki/Supplemental_Nutrition_Assistance_Program "Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program"), putting local merchants on a "boom or bust" cycle each month when [EBT payments](/wiki/Electronic_Benefit_Transfer "Electronic Benefit Transfer") were deposited.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

At the 2000 census, 46.1% of Woonsocket's population were identified as being of [French](/wiki/French_people "French people") or [French-Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") ethnic heritage. The city has referred to itself as *La ville la plus française aux États-Unis*, 'the most French city in the United States'.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
